text: The surgical team uses robotic assistance to perform a stapleless end colostomy reversal. Four 8.5mm ports are placed in a straight line to access the left lower quadrant and pelvis. The procedure begins with lysis of adhesions and removal of attachments around the colostomy in the pelvis, followed by preparation of the pelvic area for anastomosis. The previously placed suture is identified.

The surgeon identifies the peritoneal reflection and performs limited dissection to preserve blood supply. Using a bipolar energy device, they divide the attachments, scar tissue, and inflamed tissue. The descending colon is divided distally to facilitate an end-to-end anastomosis without tension.

The surgeon replaces the SASE suture using a 3-0 barbed suture in a running fashion, starting from the back wall. The view is switched to ICG mode to assess the blood supply during the anastomosis, with ICG administered intravenously. The Cornell suture technique is employed to ensure continuous evaluation of blood supply due to the patient's history of ischemic colon and vascular disease.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the anastomosis, ensuring no gaps are present, and locks the sutures on both sides. The anastomosis is tested by filling the pelvis with water and performing a sigmoidoscopy, confirming no leaks. The distal end remnant is placed in an endobag and extracted through the liver port. The stoma is closed by excising the colostomy area, closing the fascia, and applying surgical glue to the skin and soft tissue. The port placement and extraction site are visible.
